sentences.text: Yes.
My full name is Aranka, Rosner Siegal.
Rosner is my mother's maiden name.
But I had decided to use it since there, there is nobody left to use the name Rosner.
My father's last name was Davidovitz, Ignac Davidovitz.
I was born in Hungary, which is really the Carpathian part of Hungary, very near to Romania, Ukraine.
And now it is Ukraine.
Beregszasz.
Oh, that's a good one.
B-E-R-G-S-Z-A-S-Z.
It's sort of a difficult spelling.
And even I am out of the Hungarian spelling right now, having been here for so long.
Yes.
I was actually born in that house, delivered by a midwife, and lived there till age 13, when the Germans had taken us away.
The date of my birthday is June 10, 1930.
My mother's first name was Rise Seren.
She had two first names.
Rise was more her Jewish name.
And Seren was what was on her birth certificate.
Well, my father was Ignac Davidovitz.
But actually, he was my stepfather.
My father had died when I was nine months old.
And my mother had remarried.
And he had adopted me.
So I had always looked upon him as my father, not having remembered my own father since I was only nine months old, and also because he was the kindest father that could have ever been.
My friends envied me because I had such a wonderful father.
And I was not told about him being my stepfather for many years.
And then I found out from a neighbor.
And I was very upset that my mother failed to tell me that.
Maybe she waited too long.
But she just felt the relationship was so good that she was afraid to spoil it.
But to me, he was my father.
Oh, I must have been nine.
Oh, yes.
I had five sisters and a brother.
I was actually the fifth girl in the family.
And after my mother remarried, she had two more children.
And after five girls we had a boy and then a little girl.
OK, my oldest's name was Lilli, or Louisa on her birth certificate.
And Roszi was next to the oldest.
Then it was Etu, who has survived and lives in Israel.
And my sister, Violet, who is here in the States, she has survived with me.
And then it's me.
And after me is Sandorn, my little brother, and my little sister Joli, who was only four years old when they took us away from home.
Yes.
Yes.
I went to school, but unfortunately only to fifth grade because of the war.
I went to Hungarian school, which was a public school.
And the Jewish children were no longer allowed to go to public school after my fifth grade, which is really when I was, I guess, 11 years old.
By age 12, we had a makeshift school set up by the Jewish teachers, who had also been let go.
And this was in the temple, the Jewish temple, the big temple in Beregszasz, which was just a big, beautiful temple.
I happen to have a photograph of it.
And--
It was called the big temple, the big temple on Main Street, because the others were all just little neighborhood temples.
And this one was on Main Street and a huge, beautiful building.
And they had set up this makeshift school, but of course, they just have two classrooms.
So we were children of various ages.
And it was very difficult to teach there because, you know, we were on different levels and also because the children weren't that well behaved because they had all of this tension outside.
And they really couldn't settle down that much at school.
So I would call my makeshift school as just a place to go and be with other children because we really didn't learn that much.
The public school was a two-story building.
And we went there from first grade to eighth grade.
[DRINKS]
Not really far.
It was a good walk.
In the wintertime when it was cold, Hungary used to have a lot of snow.
They would just sort of dig a little path on the cement sidewalk that was just wide enough for people to walk through.
And that's how we went to school.
And I guess it would take me about 20 minutes to get to school.
Many of times we ran the  last, last couple of blocks because we would already hear the school bells, you know.
And we would take off and run.
And also in the wintertime-- I have to tell you something very interesting.
My mother would get up early, and she'd bake potatoes.
And she gave us each two potatoes to put in our pocket to keep our hands warm while we were walking to school because it was such a long way.
And then, of course, later on we could eat it.
But I just never heard of anybody else doing this, of giving the children baked potatoes as they send them off.
I mean, this was besides our lunch.
This was not our lunch.
But it was comfortable, you know, comforting to have these warm pockets.
No.
We were the only ones.
And kids would always ask us, you know, can they have a piece?
And it was kind of fun.
Yes, we were two years apart.
Well, Lilli was the only one that was four years older than Roszi.
But Roszi was born in 1924.
Etu was born in 1926.
[NON-ENGLISH] was born-- whose name now is Violet-- she was born in 1928.
And I was born in 1930.
But then because of the remarriage and so forth, there was again, a bigger age difference between my little brother and myself.
I was 13 when they took us from home, and he was just six.
And the little sister was four.
Well, it had marble stairs.
And that was always noisy, kids running up and down the stairs, you know, in and out of recess or coming in.
And-- but it was a good school.
And they tried to teach us as much as they can.

But when I go to schools now-- as you know, I do a lot of speaking-- and I see what the children are learning today, I cannot believe it, because it was no where as good as school or as beneficial as far as, you know, learning.
We learned reading, writing, arithmetic.
Oh, and we had Hebrew once a week.
Once a week we had a Hebrew lesson.
We had a Hebrew teacher who would come in.
And we would go to a particular classroom, just as the Catholic children went to one classroom.
Catholic children went to their classroom.
Yeah, we had separate classroom.
We would divide up for religious instructions.
Jewish and Catholic, yes.
We had some Protestants as well.
But it was mostly Catholic, yeah-- Russian Orthodox even.
That was once a week.
Oh, yes.
Yes, but it wasn't really bad.
It wasn't anything that bothered us that much.
We just kind of shrugged it off, you know, as part of mischief, or name-calling, or whatever.
It didn't really bother us that much till all this thing started with Hitler.
Well, I remember once being chased.
But that was already with coming into Hitler's time.
I was being chased by some Christian boys.
I was coming home kind of late because my sister and I both stopped at the lending library after school.
And it was dark.
It was winter.
So I guess that was the winter of 1943.
And as we were walking home, we saw the Jewish men with their prayers shawls on running out of the synagogue because the synagogue was right close to the street.
And we could see them running out of the synagogue.
And then we saw these young hoodlums with sticks chasing them.
And my sister and I stopped and we said, stop that.
What are you doing?
You know, yelling at them that they shouldn't chase these old men, who were the only ones that really at that point took a chance on going to the temple.
Because it was a critical time and dangerous for the Jews by then.
And they said, oh, look who's telling us what to do, two little Jew bitches.
Did you recognize the hoodlums?
We recognized one of them that was my sister's age.
She was at that time about 15.
One of her classmates.
So I said, we're going to go tell the police.
And he said, what police are you going to tell this to, as if they cared if we beat up on some old Jews?
[DRINKS] And then they started chasing us.
But a man who was walking towards us had gotten in between them and us.
And that gave us a little time, you know, so we could run away.
And he sort of stopped them.
But we came in, I mean, panting and soaking wet, even though it was the middle of the winter, you know, all perspired, and frightened, and all that.
And it really got my mother upset.
And my older sister was there with her two children-- with her child-- excuse me-- the other child was really my little sister that she was taking care of.
So she had one child in her arm, and that was my little sister.
And she got very, very upset.
And she helped us off with our coats and asked us to tell her what happened.
But as I said, till all that started with Hitler, of course there was always anti-Semitism and name calling.
In the little village where I spent my summer with my grandmother, it was the same thing.
I played with the kids.
But then every now and then-- and it was remarkable.
It was always the boys.
This was Komnaty.
And that was Ukraine at the time, whereas we were Hungarian.
And it was just an hour's train ride.
And we spent all our summer with my grandmother, who lived on a farm.
And she owned a lot of land at the time.
And she had like sharecroppers.
And we used to have great fun there because we could just run wild in the fields, and pick berries and mushrooms in the forest, which was very nearby.
And it was an entirely different life, just so much freer.
Not only didn't we have the school, but we didn't have my mother's discipline.
And my Bubbe, as we called her, was very busy in the field.
So she more or less let us run loose.

And also, we learned Ukraine, which was very good-- Ukraine from the Ukraine people who lived there, who were Russian Orthodox, and Yiddish from my grandmother, because we didn't speak Yiddish in the house.
And even though Grandma could speak a perfect Hungarian, she would pretend not to understand a word if we spoke in Hungarian, just so we would speak to her in Yiddish.
So we actually learned two languages while we were there.
And we learned German in the school, a little German.
So we had German, Hungarian, Ukraine, and Yiddish at that point.
And she was Bubbe Rosner?
Yes, my mother's mother, my maternal grandmother.
And she had the biggest influence on me, I think, in my life.
As a matter of fact, in my books that I have written, I speak mostly about Bubbe, which is sort of strange, especially the first book.
And I think because she was so different.
And she was strict in her own way, too.
But she was very religious, so very different from my mother.
And she always talked about God.
And she just presented life to us in a very different way then we ever saw anybody else in the city, you know, believe and do.
And her morals were just so strict.
And she was always there for her neighbors, helping to deliver children.
And I mean this for the Christian neighbors as well.
They all loved her.
If somebody was sick, she was like the medicine lady, you know.
They would call on her.
And she always knew what to do.
Usually we took turns because Grandma was a widow.
And my mother never wanted her to be by herself.
So we took turns being with her.
Her constant companion was really my sister, Roszi.
My older sister really was married.
And Roszi, by the time they took us away, was 20.
But she really went to live with my grandmother when she was 16.
She loved the farm.
And she really did it all of her free choice, but also because my mother didn't want Grandma to be alone.
And she was the one that really wanted to stay with her.
In the summer, then Roszi would go home to the city.
This gave her a chance to go home to the city.
And we went one at a time mostly.
But then on the holidays, everybody would come.
We celebrated the holidays with Grandma.
Even though we weren't terribly religious like Grandma was, we had a traditional home.
And we were very much involved in the celebration of the holidays.
That sort of marked the different important parts of the year for us.
Oh, for the whole summer.
For the whole summer, yep.
As soon as school was out, we would be packed up and go to my grandmother's.
So was my other sister, Violet, who was just two years older than myself.
But she would get very homesick.
So she never stayed too long.
Yeah, because I really wasn't homesick.
It isn't that I didn't miss my parents or the two little ones, you know.
Well, there wasn't always the two little ones.
But I just liked it so much there, you know.
I liked the children in the country.
We ran around barefoot.
Made friends right away, learned the language right away.
Non-Jewish friends.
Well, I had some Jewish friends too.
There were some Jewish friends.
My closest friend across the street was Jewish.
And-- but the other children I played with in the field were just mostly the children of the farmers.
Well, they went to school.
They weren't as literate as we were.
As a matter of fact, one year I was stuck there for a whole school year.
And I went to their school.
And from the very first day on-- this was at the time that the borders were closed.
That's why I was stuck there, because the Hungarians had taken over this little village.
That was in 1939.
And the Hungarians came into this little village.
And there was a, oh, a terrible, terrible fighting going on, lots of bodies in the river.
Yes.
It's K-A-N-Y-A-N-T, Komnaty.
I think I'm spelling it right.
I think I maybe we should check one of the books.
I hope I am.
Anyhow, because I'm not spelling it the way we used to spell it over there in the book.
That's why I'm a little bit concerned, because we used to spell it in a Hungarian fashion.
And now we're spelling it so it would sound the same in English.
It was my publisher's idea.
Oh, yes.
Absolutely.
Absolutely, because my grandmother didn't differentiate.
On the Sabbath, you know, she was Jewish, and she went to the temple with the other Jewish women.
And I say women, because they sat separately, you know.
In those synagogues, we didn't have reform there.
So as a matter of fact, in that little village, you only had one kind.
That was really like orthodox.
And the women sat separately from the men in the temple.
And you know, she was very strict about the Sabbath with me too.
And then she did all her Jewish things.
And at night, she always read her Jewish prayer books for hours, even though she used to tell me she knew it by heart.
But she kept the book open just in case she forgot a word here or there.
She knew all her prayers by heart.
But-- so she was a religious lady.
But during the day, she was with the farmers.
And I mean they were friends.
And they ate their lunch together.
I'd bring a little basket, you know, of food.
Yes, she was born in that very house.
And I understand when they came to get her, she put up quite a fight.
She didn't want to leave.
She said, no.
This is the house I was born in.
I lived here all my life.
And nobody's going to take me away.
And she absolutely refused to budge.
Yes.
She raised my mother there.
But my mother got married at 16.
She got married very, very young.
And she was very anxious to live in the city.
It was just one hour on a coal train.
Chug, chug, chug.
Coal train, yeah.
And, you know, it was a very slow train compared to ours.
So it would probably be a half an hour here.
But it was an hour's train ride.
Beregszasz?
Yeah.
As a matter of fact--
I can describe it from memory.
But just very recently, one of my readers had gone to Ukraine to visit his old mother, who still lives here-- lives there rather.
And he's a professor from Virginia, and Christian.
And he wrote to me that he was going to the Ukraine.

And having read my books, he was so impressed with my books that he is going to go, if it's OK with me, and retrace my description of the city and this little village, and bring me back pictures if he can.
[DRINKS]
So I have all my memory photographs, you know, of the place.
And then just very recently he took some photographs that he brought-- sent to me, actually, of my house.
And, of course, at first glance I said, it's not my house.
And then I didn't tell anything my sister about it.
But when she came to visit that following weekend, I just showed her the photograph.
And she looked and looked, and she said, what is this?
And I said, that's our house.
That's not our house, same exact reaction as I, because it is so dilapidated.
They have not painted it in 50 years.
It has the same coat of paint on it.
But he said the same city, the whole city of Beregszasz is like that, very dilapidated.
And he said he doesn't think that there was a new house built in the city in 50 years.
Can you picture it?
Could you describe the inside of your house?
Yes.
The inside of our house-- the house was very straight up to the sidewalk, that type of a house, you know.
It didn't sit back.
The courtyard was to the right side as you faced the house.
Oh, yes.
We had not too many rooms.
We had my parent's large bedroom, and then one room that was called the salon.
It was fixed up as such.
But it also served as a guest room.
Or, if one of the children was sick and needed to be separated from the others, so they wouldn't all catch it, they slept in the salon because it had like a sofa bed in there.
And then we had a huge kitchen, which was kitchen, and dining room, and entertainment center, and all that sort of stuff.
And that was the center of the house.
This huge kitchen actually set in the center of the house.
And then beyond the kitchen was the children's room on the other side of the kitchen.

In other words, there were two bedrooms in the front of the house, the kitchen in the middle, and then another bedroom, and then a summer kitchen, which was a little open-- well, not open, but a kitchen that would be very cool in the summertime where my mother cooked-- and a huge porch that ran the whole length of the house, inside, you know.
Because to the front, we just had the three large windows.
And the two bedrooms faced the outside.
But as you walked into the courtyard, the house was very long, even though, you know, it looks small from the street.
And that entire length had a porch.
And then on the right-hand side was my mother's flowers.
She had a flower garden.
And then there was an area where we would have a sukkah on the sukkahs.
And also-- AS (ON ANSWERING MACHINE): Hello.
This is Aranka.
We can't speak to you just now.
